title: Pet Urine on Carpet
timeEstimate: 30 minutes to 24 hours depending on enzyme dwell; pro-lane for pad contamination
stainChemistry: Pet urine is more concentrated than human urine — higher urea, uric acid, pheromones, and protein. The special failure mode is uric acid crystallization: as the urine dries, uric acid forms insoluble crystals that lodge in fiber and carpet pad. Those crystals don't just sit there — they reactivate with humidity, bacteria metabolize them, and the smell returns days or weeks after "cleaning." Cat urine is the hardest variant because of felinine (a sulfur amino acid unique to cats) that bacterially decomposes into mercaptans — the sharp, persistent cat-pee smell. Ammonia-based cleaners are a trap: they smell like urine to a pet, which triggers re-marking. Vinegar + baking soda home-remedies cancel each other chemically and do effectively nothing. The actual unlock is a pet-specific enzyme cleaner that digests uric acid.
whyThisWorks: Enzyme cleaner digests uric acid crystals — no other common chemistry does. Saturation matters more than product: the enzyme has to reach wherever the urine reached, which on carpet usually means through the pile and into the pad. Spraying the surface does almost nothing. Cold water, Dawn, and covering the treated area to keep the enzyme wet are the mechanics that actually deliver the enzyme to the crystal.
defaultAssumption: Cat or dog accident. Fresh + caught-in-the-act = high-confidence DIY. Dried overnight or older = home chemistry still works but needs full 24-hour enzyme dwell. Blacklight-still-glowing after two full enzyme rounds = pad contamination = pro sub-surface extraction.

spottingProtocol.agent: UV Blacklight — Find the Whole Spot
spottingProtocol.equipment: UV blacklight (cheap on Amazon), painter's tape
spottingProtocol.instruction: In a dark room, sweep a UV blacklight over the area. Urine glows pale yellow-green. Mark edges with painter's tape. Pet urine almost always spreads farther than the visible stain — treating too small is the #1 reason the odor returns and the pet re-marks. If no blacklight, treat 4 inches outside the visible edge on all sides.
Dampen a white towel with cold water. Press hard on the spot. Lift, flip, repeat. Never pour — over-wetting carries urine deeper into the pad. Weighted dry towel 3–5 min to extract surface moisture.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn dish soap + cold water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dawn on a damp white cloth, press on the spot 5 min, blot. Clears surface fats and salts so the enzyme (next step) can reach uric acid crystals underneath. Do NOT use OxiClean or alkaline cleaners — they can set protein.

spottingProtocol.agent: Enzyme Cleaner — Saturate to Pad Depth
spottingProtocol.equipment: Pet-specific enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle / Rocco & Roxie / Anti Icky Poo), damp towel, plastic wrap, tape
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply 2–3× the volume of urine you estimate reached the area. The enzyme must reach wherever the urine reached, so saturate through the pile to pad depth on pre-dried accidents. Cover with a damp white towel + plastic wrap taped at the edges to keep the enzyme wet. Fresh: 30–60 min. Overnight accident: 24 hours under cover.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Enzymes deactivated by heat, chlorine bleach, and strong alkali. Don't layer chemistry. No steam cleaning during or before enzyme step.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Uncover. Stack paper towels, weight down, leave overnight to wick residual moisture from the pad. Fan the area fully dry.

Nose to pile in daylight. Blacklight in a dark room. Still smells or still glows = repeat steps 4–5 once more with more enzyme, longer dwell. Still positive after 2 full rounds = pad contamination = pro sub-surface extraction.

steps: 1. Blacklight scan + tape the full contaminated area.
3. Dawn / NSD damp cloth to clear surface residue.
4. Pet enzyme — saturate to pad depth. Cover + dwell 30 min–24 hr.
5. Paper towel + weight overnight extract. Fan-dry.
6. Re-blacklight + smell check. Pad contamination = pro.
products: Pet enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle / Rocco & Roxie / Anti Icky Poo)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; no heat until enzyme cycle complete
warnings: NEVER ammonia-based cleaners — triggers pet re-marking
NEVER hot water / steam — sets protein + deactivates future enzyme rounds
NEVER chlorine bleach — deactivates enzymes and damages carpet
NEVER OxiClean before enzyme — alkaline can set protein
Over-wetting carries urine into pad — tamp, don't pour
Blacklight-positive after 2 full rounds = pad contamination = pro

homeSolutions.agent: Find the Whole Spot — Blacklight It
homeSolutions.instruction: Pet urine almost always spreads farther than you can see. Get a cheap UV blacklight off Amazon (under $15). In a dark room, sweep it across the area — urine glows pale yellow-green. Mark the edges with painter's tape. Without a blacklight, treat at least 4 inches beyond the visible edge on every side. Treating too small is the #1 reason odor returns and your pet re-marks.

homeSolutions.agent: The Two Rules — No Heat, No Ammonia
homeSolutions.instruction: Two hard rules before you touch anything: (1) NO hot water, no steam, no dryer — heat sets the protein permanently and bakes the odor in, and (2) NO ammonia-based cleaners — they smell like urine to your pet and invite re-marking. Skip the vinegar+baking soda combo from Pinterest — they cancel each other chemically and don't touch uric acid. The real workhorse is an enzyme cleaner made for pets.

Fresh spot: stack paper towels, step on them to wick urine up, replace until towels come up dry. Dried / crusty: lay a COLD damp white towel on the spot for 10 minutes to rehydrate, then blot. Don't scrub dried crystals — mashing them into the fiber spreads the odor. No pouring water on the carpet.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Cold Water — Clears the Way for Enzyme
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix a tablespoon of Dawn with two cups of cold water. Dab onto the area with a white cloth, wait 5 minutes, blot with a clean damp cloth. Dawn lifts surface fat and salts so the enzyme in step 5 can actually reach the uric-acid crystals underneath. Do NOT use OxiClean, baking soda, or alkaline cleaners — they can set the protein.

Apply a pet-specific enzyme cleaner — Nature's Miracle, Rocco & Roxie, Simple Solution, or Anti Icky Poo. Read the label, but most work best when you SATURATE the area to the pad, not spray the surface. Use 2–3× the volume of urine you think soaked in. The enzyme has to reach wherever the urine reached. This is the single most important step — the one that actually digests the uric acid.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Enzymes are deactivated by heat, chlorine bleach, and strong alkaline cleaners. Don't mix. Don't steam before, during, or after.

homeSolutions.agent: Cover and Dwell — Keep It Wet
homeSolutions.instruction: Lay a damp (not dripping) white towel over the treated area and cover with plastic wrap. Tape the edges down. This keeps the enzyme wet instead of drying on the surface. Fresh spots: 30–60 minutes. Older or overnight spots: a full 24 hours. The enzyme is literally digesting the uric acid and needs time to finish.

Uncover. Stack paper towels over the spot, weigh them down with a heavy book, leave overnight to wick residual moisture out of the pad. Point a fan at it. Don't vacuum until fully dry. A damp carpet pad is mold territory.

homeSolutions.agent: Smell + Blacklight Check — Two Rounds Max
homeSolutions.instruction: Nose right down into the pile in daylight. Blacklight in a dark room. Smell gone + no fluorescence = win. Still positive = one more round of steps 5–6 with more enzyme and a full 24-hour dwell. Still positive after TWO full rounds = the urine is in the pad or subfloor, and home chemistry is at its ceiling.

Tell the pro: "Pet urine on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, blacklight still positive after two enzyme rounds (Nature's Miracle / Rocco & Roxie). No heat, no ammonia. Possible pad or subfloor contamination." They'll run a sub-surface extraction tool that flushes the pad with clean water at pressure; worst case the pad gets cut out and replaced. That handoff saves them diagnostic time and prevents them from starting over.

steps: 1. Blacklight-scan + tape the full area; treat 4" beyond visible edge.
2. Two rules: NO hot water / steam / dryer. NO ammonia cleaners.
3. Blot fresh or rehydrate dried with cold damp towel.
4. Dawn + cold water damp cloth, 5 min. No OxiClean.
5. Pet enzyme — SATURATE to pad depth, 2–3× urine volume.
6. Cover with damp towel + plastic wrap. Dwell 30 min (fresh) or 24 hr (older).
8. Re-blacklight + smell. 2 rounds max — then pro sub-surface extraction.
products: Pet enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle / Rocco & Roxie / Anti Icky Poo / Simple Solution)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if blacklight/smell stays positive after 2 full enzyme rounds — pad or subfloor is contaminated, home chemistry can't reach it.
warnings: NEVER hot water / steam / dryer — sets protein + kills future enzyme rounds
NEVER OxiClean / alkaline before enzyme — can set protein
Never pour — saturation is enzyme-delivery, not carpet-flooding
Blacklight-positive after 2 rounds = pro sub-surface extraction

neverDo: Never hot water, steam, or dryer on pet urine
Never OxiClean or alkaline before enzyme step
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester carpet: tolerates full protocol
Wool carpet: pH-sensitive — pro route, enzyme selection matters

materialWarnings: Pad contamination is the real ceiling — pro sub-surface extraction, not truck-mount

escalation.when: Blacklight or smell positive after 2 full enzyme rounds, large area, cat urine with persistent felinine odor, wool carpet, or suspected subfloor contamination.
escalation.whatToTell: "Pet urine on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, [cat / dog], age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme ([brand]) × 2 rounds. Blacklight still positive. No heat, no ammonia. Possible pad or subfloor contamination — sub-surface extraction needed."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with sub-surface extraction tool (truck-mount is NOT the same)

contextQuestions: Cat or dog? (cat urine is harder — felinine + mercaptans)
Has anyone used an ammonia cleaner, vinegar-baking soda, or steam yet?
Is this a one-off accident or a recurring spot (pad saturation suspicion)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Cat urine with felinine is the hardest variant; budget a full 24-hour enzyme cycle even for fresh. Recurring same-spot accidents almost always mean pad contamination — skip home rounds 3+ and go pro. Wool carpet always pro-lane.
